title: Chapter 20: The Canary Sings
content: The light from her phone screen cast her face in pale blue, like a swimmer surfacing from deep water.

Aline sat cross-legged on her bed, the same bed she'd slept in since before any of this started — before the whispers and the streams and the Phantom who found her in the dark. No ring light. No stylist. No publicist hovering off-camera with index cards and panic. Just her, the cracked phone she'd never replaced, and the earpiece pressed so far into her canal it hurt.
Two hundred viewers. Then two thousand. Then the counter began climbing like a scream building in a closed room.
"Hey." Her voice came out smaller than she wanted. She cleared her throat. "Hey. I have something to tell you about my music."

The viewer count hit fifty thousand. A hundred. The comments were already a river — hearts, question marks, crying emojis, people typing WHERE HAVE YOU BEEN and ALINE OMG and the occasional is this real? She could hear the virtual roar of them, a sound without sound, a pressure against her skull like standing too close to the speakers at a stadium show.
Cass's voice slipped into her ear, low and steady as a heartbeat. "You're doing great. Don't name him yet. Let him think he's safe."

She almost laughed. Let him think he's safe. As if Marcus Webb had ever felt anything but safe. As if the man who owned half the industry's veins had ever once imagined the blood might turn against him.

She pulled a breath. The apartment smelled like cold coffee and the lavender candle Mags had left on the windowsill, and underneath it all, the faint mineral tang of the rain that had stopped an hour ago — her window was still cracked, letting the wet city air bleed through the screen.
"I haven't been honest with you," she said. "Not all of you. Not about where the music comes from. Not about who makes it with me."

The comments detonated. She watched them blur past, too fast to read, a waterfall of text and emojis and fractured speculation. The viewer count kept climbing — five hundred thousand, seven hundred thousand, and still rising like the tide.

"When I was nobody — when I was playing open mics for twelve people and uploading songs that got two hundred streams if I was lucky — someone found me." She paused. Let the silence hold. In her ear, Cass said nothing, which meant she was doing exactly what they'd planned. "An anonymous producer. He heard something in my voice that no one else heard. He believed in me before I believed in myself."
She looked directly into the camera. Into the cracked glass and the tiny lens and the two million eyes now pressed against it like faces at a window.
"He's a genius. The best producer I've ever worked with. And he's been hiding — not because he did something wrong. Because someone in this industry tried to destroy him."

The comments went berserk. WHO TRIED TO DESTROY HIM and NAME THEM and PROTECT HIM AT ALL COSTS and the hashtag started forming even as she watched — #WhoIsThePhantom, climbing the trending list like smoke up a chimney.
She paused. Let that land. Let the silence be a bell.
"I'm not going to name that person. Not yet." The words tasted like glass and honey — cutting and sweet at once. "But I want everyone to know: my producer is real. He's alive. And he's coming back."
She ended the stream.

The silence hit her like a wall. One moment: the electric roar of two million strangers pressing against the screen. The next: her bedroom, quiet, the rain-soaked air through the window, the distant murmur of traffic on the avenue below. Her hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs and felt the dampness there — sweat, she realized. She was sweating through her t-shirt.

Cass's voice returned, clipped now, businesslike, but she could hear the adrenaline singing underneath it. "It's working. #WhoIsThePhantom is number three trending. Number two. It's — Jesus, Aline. It's number one."
"But Marcus won't take this lying down."

"No. He won't." A pause. The sound of his fingers on a keyboard, rapid and precise. "He'll move fast. Counter-narrative, probably — try to paint the Phantom as a predator, a stalker, something that makes you look like a victim instead of an accomplice."
"He can try." She stood, crossed to the window. The city glittered at her, twenty floors of indifferent beauty. "But he has to find me first. And right now, the internet is looking for *him*."
Marcus Webb watched the stream from his office.

The room was dark except for the glow of the monitor — three screens, each showing a different angle of the same disaster. The Instagram Live playback on the left. The trending hashtags on the right. And in the center, Aline Rivers's face, paused on the frame where she said "someone in this industry tried to destroy him," her eyes bright with something that looked terrifyingly like triumph.

He watched his own face in the dark glass of the window — the reflection of a man cycling through phases. First: curiosity. Aline Rivers, going live? Unusual. She'd been quiet for weeks. What was she playing at?
Then: comprehension. The anonymous producer. Someone tried to destroy him. The pieces clicked into place with a sound like a bone breaking.
Then: fury.

His hand closed around the arm of his chair. The leather creaked. He didn't name me, he thought. She didn't name me. But she might as well have painted a target on my chest, because the internet doesn't need a name — it needs a question. And she just gave them the biggest question of the year.
Who destroyed the Phantom?

They'd find the smear campaign. The coordinated takedowns. The testimonials from "former collaborators" that Marcus's people had manufactured and seeded across forums and subreddits three years ago. They'd find the timeline. And the timeline would lead them here. To this office. To him.
He pulled out his phone and dialed.

"Get me PR. Now. And find out who's running point on the Rivers narrative — I want options in ten minutes, not ten hours." He listened. His jaw tightened. "I don't care if she didn't name me. She put a leash around my neck and handed the other end to the internet. Fix it."

He hung up. On the screen, Aline's frozen face stared back at him — no makeup, cracked phone, radical transparency — and for the first time in a very long time, Marcus Webb felt the cold ghost of fear walk up his spine.
Her phone rang eleven minutes after the stream ended.
"What did you *do*?"

Mags's voice was half shriek, half laugh, the sound of someone watching a car chase and not being able to look away. Aline could picture her — standing in her own kitchen probably, phone pressed to her ear, one hand Braille-reading the trending topics on her tablet.
"I started a war," Aline said.

She was still at the window. The city breathed below her, millions of people who didn't know yet that the ground had shifted. The air through the screen was cold against her face — the rain-left cold, the kind that seeps into your skin and stays. She could hear a siren somewhere in the distance, thin and rising, and the bass-heavy thump of music from an apartment three floors down, and underneath it all, her own pulse in her ears.
"And you think you can win?" Mags asked.

Aline looked down at the street. Twenty floors below, the sidewalks were wet and silver in the streetlight, the traffic slow and unremarkable. And there — standing in the shadow of the bus shelter across the road, collar turned up, phone in hand — a man. He wasn't waiting for a bus. He wasn't looking at his phone. He was looking up. At her window.
Marcus's people. She was sure of it the way you're sure of weather — not because you see the lightning but because you can feel the charge in the air.
"I don't think," she said. "I know. Because he's not just fighting for me anymore. I'm fighting with him."
A silence on the line. Then Mags, quieter: "Be careful, Aline."
"I'm done being careful." She watched the man on the street shift his weight, crack his neck, go back to staring upward. "Careful is what got us here. I'm being *deliberate*."
She hung up. Drew the curtain. Let the blue darkness of the apartment swallow her whole.
Three hours later, Cass was inside.

The Vantage Entertainment network sprawled like a city seen from above — servers branching into directories, directories into subdirectories, the architecture of a billion-dollar machine built on secrets and silence. VERA mapped the topology for him in real-time, her voice a calm thread through the labyrinth.
"Northeast quadrant, second partition. The ghost account's root access should be nested under legacyadmin_047."

He typed. The keyboard clicks were the only sound in the Nest — a soft, percussive rhythm, like rain on a tin roof. His screens lit his face from below, painting him in the colors of the data he was stealing: green, amber, the occasional flash of warning red.

Marcus was busy. Aline had seen to that. The PR crisis was consuming every hand at Vantage — the denials, the carefully worded statements, the frantic calls to journalists who were already running the story anyway. While they scrambled, Cass worked. The firewalls that should have stopped him were understaffed, the monitoring systems overwhelmed by the surge of traffic that Aline's stream had generated. It was like robbing a bank while the alarms were already screaming about something else.

"Got it." The root access prompt blinked at him, a cursor pulsing like a heartbeat. He was in. He could override Marcus's directives, reclaim the ghost account, sever the connection that had weaponized his own system against him. Three keystrokes and it would be done.
He positioned his fingers over the keys.
And stopped.

The account log was open. He'd had to scroll through it to find the override controls, and now his eyes were catching on entries that didn't belong. Not Marcus's syntax — Marcus was blunt, corporate, his commands reading like memos from a man who'd never learned to talk to machines. These were different. Tighter. More elegant. The work of someone who spoke code the way poets speak language — fluently, with an ear for structure.

Messages. Not commands. Messages embedded in the log like fossils in limestone, each one addressed to Marcus Webb's private relay. Information. About Cass. About Aline. About the Nest's location, its security protocols, its vulnerabilities. Someone had been feeding Marcus a lifeline of intelligence, drop by drop, for weeks.
Cass scrolled. His mouth had gone dry. The screen threw green light against his face, and he could feel his own pulse in his fingertips where they rested on the keyboard, cold and fast.
He found the last message. Timestamped two hours ago — right as Aline's stream went live, right as the world tilted on its axis.
> She went live. Phase two ready. Execute on your signal.
Signed with a single initial.
He stared at it. The cursor blinked. The Nest hummed around him, the servers' white noise suddenly oppressive, like the silence after a door closes and you realize you're not alone in the room.
D.

Not Marcus. Not one of Marcus's people — they signed with employee IDs, corporate drones in corporate formation. This was someone else. Someone who knew about the ghost account, about the Nest, about Aline's plans before she executed them. Someone who'd been inside this operation the whole time, watching from a position Cass hadn't even known existed.

Phase two. What was phase two? The stream was phase one — he and Aline had designed it together, built the narrative, chosen the timing. But phase two? That wasn't theirs. That belonged to someone else. Someone who'd been preparing for this moment, waiting for Aline to make her move so they could make theirs.

Cass pulled his hands from the keyboard. The override sat untouched, three keystrokes away from completion. He should execute it. He should reclaim the account, close the door, burn the access behind him. That was the mission. That was the plan.

But the plan was built on a foundation that had just cracked open, and underneath it was something he hadn't seen coming. Something that knew his name and knew where he lived and knew what he was going to do before he did it.
VERA's voice, soft and measured: "Cass? The override is ready."
"Not yet." His voice sounded strange in his own ears — hollow, distant, like someone speaking from the far end of a long corridor. "Not yet. I need to understand what I'm looking at first."
He leaned back. The chair creaked. The screens cast their cold light, and the ghost account's logs stretched behind the last message like a road leading somewhere he hadn't planned to go.
D.
Who the hell was D?
And what happened when phase two began?
